name: Vía Verde de la Sierra Trail
name_original: Via Verde de Puerto Serrano a Coripe — ida y vuelta por el Río Guadalete
description: Hike the Vía Verde de la Sierra Trail, a moderate 11.3-mile route through Andalusia's scenic landscapes, featuring tunnels and viaducts.
region_intro: Hiking around El Coronil offers a diverse landscape characterized by rolling agricultural fields, Mediterranean forest ecosystems, and the historic Vía Verde de la Sierra. The region features gentle inclines and descents across its countryside, providing varied terrain for outdoor activities. Cork oaks, wild olive trees, and riverine vegetation are common, alongside distant views of the Sierra Sur of Seville. Best hiking trails around El Coronil The most popular hiking route is Sierra de San Pablo y Montellano — circular from the population of Montellano, a 6.5 miles (10.5 km) trail that takes 3 hours 8 minutes to complete, offering views of the Sierra de San Pablo. Another top favourite among local hikers is Subida Castillo de Cote — circular by the Sierra de San Pablo, a moderate 5.0 miles (8.1 km) path. This route explores the Sierra de San Pablo, featuring varied terrain and historical points of interest. Local hikers also love the Vía Verde de la Sierra Trail, a 11.3 miles (18.2 km) trail leading through a disused railway line with tunnels and viaducts, often completed in about 4 hours 48 minutes. Hiking around El Coronil is defined by agricultural landscapes, rolling hills, and the Vía Verde de la Sierra. The network offers options for both easy and moderate ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 50 hikers have used komoot to explore El Coronil's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty of hiking trails around El Coronil?
A: Hiking trails around El Coronil offer a range of difficulties, primarily focusing on easy to moderate routes. The region's landscape features rolling agricultural fields and gentle inclines, making many paths accessible. Out of the 12 available routes, 2 are considered easy, and 10 are moderate, ensuring options for various fitness levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes in El Coronil?
A: You can expect diverse terrain, from the compacted earth and asphalt surfaces of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009104432/via-verde-de-la-sierra-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde de la Sierra Trail&lt;/a&gt; to paths through Mediterranean forest ecosystems like Dehesa de Pilares. Many routes traverse agricultural lands, including fields of wheat, sunflowers, and olive groves, with gentle inclines and descents characteristic of the rolling hills.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks to see on hikes near El Coronil?
A: Yes, several hikes offer opportunities to explore historical landmarks. For instance, the 14th-century Moorish &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5323927" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de las Aguzaderas&lt;/a&gt; is located about 3 kilometers from El Coronil and can be reached via rural paths. Another route, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009133391/subida-castillo-de-cote-circular-by-the-sierra-de-san-pablo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Subida Castillo de Cote — circular by the Sierra de San Pablo&lt;/a&gt;, explores the Sierra de San Pablo, featuring historical points of interest.

Q: Can I find viewpoints with scenic views on trails in El Coronil?
A: Absolutely. The region offers several scenic viewpoints. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1274572" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Purto del Acebuche Viewpoint – Puerto del Acebuche Viewpoint loop from Montellano&lt;/a&gt; is a route specifically designed to take you to the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1032251" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto del Acebuche Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009130864/sierra-de-san-pablo-y-montellano-circular-from-the-population-of-montellano" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de San Pablo y Montellano — circular from the population of Montellano&lt;/a&gt; trail offers views of the Sierra de San Pablo.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in El Coronil?
A: The best time to hike in El Coronil is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. During these periods, temperatures are milder and more comfortable for outdoor activities, and the natural landscapes are often at their most vibrant. Summers can be very hot, making early mornings or late afternoons preferable if hiking during that season.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking options in El Coronil?
A: Yes, El Coronil offers family-friendly hiking options, particularly on the easier routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009104432/via-verde-de-la-sierra-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde de la Sierra Trail&lt;/a&gt;, built on a disused railway line, is known for its accessibility and relatively flat terrain, making it suitable for families. The Dehesa de Pilares also features marked trails and botanical paths, ideal for pleasant walks with children.

Q: Are dogs allowed on hiking trails in El Coronil?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on many rural paths and trails in the El Coronil area, especially on the Vía Verde de la Sierra and through agricultural landscapes. However, it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or livestock, and to clean up after them to preserve the natural environment for everyone.

Q: Are there easy hiking trails suitable for beginners in El Coronil?
A: Yes, beginners will find suitable options in El Coronil. There are at least 2 easy routes available, which typically feature minimal elevation gain and well-defined paths. These trails provide a gentle introduction to the region's beautiful agricultural landscapes and natural areas without requiring extensive hiking experience.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy the most about hiking in El Coronil?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars. Reviewers often praise the diverse landscapes, from rolling agricultural fields to Mediterranean forests, and the historical elements like the Vía Verde de la Sierra. The well-maintained paths and opportunities for wildlife observation, such as griffon vultures at Peñón de Zaframagón, are also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there circular hiking routes available in El Coronil?
A: Yes, there are several circular hiking routes in the El Coronil area. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009130864/sierra-de-san-pablo-y-montellano-circular-from-the-population-of-montellano" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de San Pablo y Montellano — circular from the population of Montellano&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009133391/subida-castillo-de-cote-circular-by-the-sierra-de-san-pablo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Subida Castillo de Cote — circular by the Sierra de San Pablo&lt;/a&gt; are popular options that allow you to start and finish in the same location.

Q: Can I spot any wildlife while hiking in El Coronil?
A: Yes, the region offers opportunities for wildlife spotting. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009104432/via-verde-de-la-sierra-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt; passes by the Peñón de Zaframagón, which hosts one of Europe's most populated griffon vulture reserves. The Dehesa de Pilares, a Mediterranean forest, is also home to small animals like rabbits, partridges, and various reptiles and amphibians.

Q: Are there any trails that pass by water features or reservoirs?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009092247/torre-del-aguila-reservoir-and-canada-real-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torre del Águila Reservoir and Cañada Real Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that takes you past the Torre del Águila Reservoir, offering scenic views of the water. The Vía Verde de la Sierra also features riverine vegetation along its path.
tips: Beginning of the Vía Verda that goes in the direction of Coripe and where there is a place to stop and spend some time. The town of Puerto Serrano is a few meters from the start of the greenway.

Path that is a greenway that comes from the town of Puerto Serrano and that circulates following the Río Guadalete avoiding the different hills in the area called "cerros", in the Sierra de la Espuela.

faqs.question: What is the overall difficulty of the Vía Verde de la Sierra Trail?
faqs.answer: The Vía Verde de la Sierra Trail is generally considered easy. Its conversion from a railway line means it features a smooth, even path, making it suitable for various users, including families and recreational hikers. While mostly flat, there are some gentle inclines, particularly when traveling from Puerto Serrano towards Olvera.

faqs.question: How long does it take to hike the entire trail?
faqs.answer: The full Vía Verde de la Sierra Trail spans approximately 36 to 38 kilometers (22.5 to 24 miles). The time it takes to hike depends on your pace and whether you complete the entire length, but a typical full-day hike or multi-day trip would be appropriate.

faqs.question: Where can I park and access the trail?
faqs.answer: Trailheads are accessible at various points along the route. For instance, you can start near Puerto Serrano (Greenway). Parking is typically available at or near these access points in the towns connected by the trail.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Vía Verde de la Sierra Trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Vía Verde de la Sierra Trail is generally dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or other trail users, and to clean up after them.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to visit the trail?
faqs.answer: The best time to visit is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. During these periods, the weather is mild and pleasant for hiking, and the natural scenery is particularly vibrant. Summers can be very hot in Andalusia, and winters can be cooler and wetter.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay an entrance fee to use the trail?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay an entrance fee to access and use the Vía Verde de la Sierra Trail. It is a public recreational path.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery and landmarks can I expect to see?
faqs.answer: The trail offers diverse scenery, including dense forests, rolling hills, and views of the Sierra de Grazalema and Sierra de las Nieves mountains. You'll pass through 30 tunnels and cross four impressive viaducts. A significant highlight is the Peñón de Zaframagón, home to a large colony of griffon vultures. The route also runs parallel to the Guadalete and Guadalporcún rivers, and you can see the ancient 'Chaparro de la Vega' holm oak.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail connects several charming towns, such as Ubrique and El Bosque, where you can find cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options. These towns provide convenient stops for refreshments or overnight stays.

faqs.question: What are some unique features of this trail?
faqs.answer: The Vía Verde de la Sierra is unique for its innovative use of a former railway line, transforming it into a recreational path. Its design incorporates original tunnels and viaducts, providing a distinct sense of adventure. It was even recognized as Europe's best Greenway in 2009.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route is part of the broader Vías Verdes network in Spain. Along its course, it covers 100% of the official "Vía Verde de la Sierra" route, and also passes through sections of the "Túnel del Esqueleto" and "Túnel del Galletón".

faqs.question: Is the trail suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. The trail's generally smooth and even surface, combined with its easy difficulty, makes it very suitable for families with children. The tunnels and viaducts can also add an exciting element for younger hikers.
